title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
What is Shinylive? How does it differ from the traditional Shiny app? What are the pros and cons of Shinylive in comparison with Shiny Apps?



Shinylive is a way to run Shiny applications directly in the web browser. Instead of depending on a live R server, Shinylive uses browser-based technology so the app can run on the client side.



Traditional Shiny apps usually depend on a server. When a user interacts with the app, the request is processed on the server and the result is sent back to the browser.
Shinylive is different because the app runs in the browser itself. This makes it possible to publish the app more like a static website.





Shinylive apps can be hosted as static files, which makes them easier to publish on services like GitHub Pages.



There is no need to maintain a full Shiny server for simple projects.



Shinylive is especially useful for lightweight educational examples and demonstrations.






Not every Shiny feature works the same way in a browser-based environment.



Heavy computations may be harder to run in the browser than on a server.



Traditional Shiny is still better for apps that require databases, authentication, or large server-side resources.




Traditional Shiny is better for powerful and complex applications that need server support.
Shinylive is better for lightweight apps that are easy to publish and share online.

title: About
text:
This website was created for Module 10.
It includes:

A home page
A dashboard
An essay on Shiny apps
An essay on Shinylive



The purpose of this project is to demonstrate my understanding of how to share outputs with an audience using Quarto.

How to create dashboards
How Shiny apps work

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Assume that your coworkers are interested in learning about Shiny. Create a lecture note that teaches them what Shiny is and how it works.



Shiny is an R package that allows users to build interactive web applications directly from R. It is especially useful for data analysts and data scientists who want to share data tools, visualizations, and models through a web browser.



Shiny is important because it allows people to move beyond static graphs and reports. A Shiny app lets the audience interact with the data by choosing filters, selecting variables, and seeing results update immediately.



A traditional Shiny app usually has two main parts.


The user interface controls what the audience sees in the browser. This includes titles, plots, tables, buttons, sliders, and menus.



The server controls the logic of the app. It tells R how to respond when a user interacts with the app. For example, when a user selects a new variable, the server updates the graph or table.




Shiny apps can be used for:

interactive dashboards
data exploration
business reporting
teaching tools
model demonstrations





It is friendly for R users.
It helps make analysis interactive.
It is useful for teaching and presentations.
It supports dashboards and apps for decision-making.





Traditional Shiny apps usually require a server.
Large apps can become complex.
Deployment may take more technical setup than a static report.
